topics.title: Optional & Parallel Streams
topics.description: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not hold a value — a type-safe alternative to returning null that forces callers to handle the absent case. Parallel Streams split a stream into sub-streams processed concurrently by the ForkJoinPool. Use parallel() for CPU-intensive, stateless operations on large datasets. Both features are from Java 8 and transform how Java code expresses intent without imperative null checks or explicit threading.
topics.story: Optional is like a gift box: it might have something inside or be empty. Before Optional, every method that could fail returned null — callers forgot to check, NullPointerExceptions happened in production at 2am. Optional forces callers to open the box safely. Parallel streams are like a document-shredding company: instead of one person shredding 10,000 pages (sequential), you split the pile among 8 people and combine the shredded output at the end. Works great for independent tasks; terrible if workers need to talk to each other.
topics.analogy: Optional = Schrödinger's value — don't observe it directly, use map/flatMap/orElse to work with it safely. orElse = default if empty. orElseThrow = throw if empty. map = transform if present. flatMap = chain another Optional-returning function. ifPresent = side effect only if present. Parallel stream = .parallel() on any stream. ForkJoinPool splits work recursively. Thread-unsafe operations (shared state, I/O) = broken in parallel. Stateless lambdas = safe in parallel.
topics.memoryAnchor: Optional.of(v) throws NPE if null. Optional.ofNullable(v) = safe. Optional.empty(). .isPresent() / .isEmpty(). .get() (unsafe — throws if empty). .orElse(default). .orElseGet(supplier). .orElseThrow(). .map(fn). .flatMap(fn). .filter(pred). .ifPresent(action). Parallel: stream.parallel() or collection.parallelStream(). Use for: stateless CPU-bound ops on 10k+ elements. Avoid: I/O, shared state, small collections, ordered operations. parallelStream() uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool().
topics.firstPrinciples: Optional is a monad — map/flatMap chain transformations without null checks, short-circuiting on empty. Parallel streams use the Fork/Join framework: work-stealing thread pool splits the source into chunks, processes in parallel, then combines via Collector's combiner function. The overhead of splitting + combining must be less than the savings from parallelism — only worthwhile for large (10k+) independent computations.
topics.bruteForce: Null checks: if (user != null && user.getAddress() != null && user.getAddress().getCity() != null). Sequential stream for large CPU-bound transformations: one thread, one core. Manual threading with ExecutorService for parallelism.
topics.optimizedApproach: Optional chain: Optional.ofNullable(user).map(User::getAddress).map(Address::getCity).orElse('Unknown'). Parallel stream for CPU-bound stateless ops: list.parallelStream().filter(...).map(...).collect(). Custom ForkJoinPool for parallel streams needing different thread count than commonPool.
topics.whenToUse: Optional: return type for methods that may not find a result (findById, findFirst). Never use as method parameter (use overloading instead). Never use in fields or collections (overhead + serialization issues). Parallel stream: sorting/filtering large lists, CPU-heavy transformations (parsing, crypto), embarrassingly parallel algorithms. Avoid parallel for: I/O-bound work (use CompletableFuture instead), small datasets (<1000), operations with side effects, ordered streams where order matters.

examples.title: Optional — Safe Null Handling Chain
examples.explanation: map() applies a function if the Optional is present, returning a new Optional. flatMap() is for functions that themselves return Optional — prevents Optional<Optional<T>>. orElse() always evaluates its argument (even if Optional is present). orElseGet() takes a Supplier — evaluated lazily, only when Optional is empty. Use orElseGet for expensive defaults (DB calls, object creation).
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Data: findById() returns Optional<T> — standard pattern since Spring 5. DevLearner: topicRepository.findById(id).orElseThrow(TopicNotFoundException::new). Never return null from repository methods. Optional.ofNullable() when wrapping external APIs that may return null.

examples.title: Parallel Streams — When to Use and When Not To
examples.explanation: Parallel streams split the source into chunks using Spliterator, process with ForkJoinPool.commonPool() (size = CPU cores - 1), then combine results. Thread count = available processors by default. Shared mutable state (count[0]++) causes race conditions — always use stream terminal operations (count, sum, collect) or AtomicInteger. Custom ForkJoinPool wrapping bypasses commonPool — useful when the default pool is too small or already used.
examples.realWorldUse: Data processing pipelines: parallel stream to transform 100k JSON records on an 8-core machine. Image processing: parallel map to resize thumbnails. Avoid in web handlers — commonPool is shared; a slow parallel stream blocks other requests. For I/O parallelism (DB calls, HTTP), use CompletableFuture with custom executor instead.

problems.title: Optional Chain — User Address Extraction
problems.description:
Safely extract a city from a nullable user object chain using `Optional`:

**Example:**
User user = getUser(1); // may return null or have null address/city
String city = Optional.ofNullable(user)
    .map(User::getAddress)
    .map(Address::getCity)
    .orElse("Unknown");

getCity(null) => "Unknown"
getCity(User{address=null}) => "Unknown"
getCity(User{Address{city=null}}) => "Unknown"
getCity(User{Address{city="NYC"}}) => "NYC"
problems.hint1: Optional.ofNullable(user).map(u -> u.address) — each step returns Optional.
problems.hint2: Build city and country as separate Optionals, then combine.
problems.hint3: orElse("Address Unknown") as the final fallback.
problems.testCases:
Mumbai, India
London
Address Unknown
Address Unknown

problems.title: Parallel Word Frequency Count
problems.description:
Count word frequency in a large text using a parallel stream.

**Example:**
Map<String,Long> freq = Arrays.stream(bigText.split("\\s+"))
    .parallel()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingByConcurrent(w -> w.toLowerCase(), Collectors.counting()));

Parallel vs sequential on 1M words: parallel ~3x faster on 4-core machine.

**Key insight:** Use `groupingByConcurrent` (thread-safe) not `groupingBy` with parallel streams.
problems.hint2: Character.toLowerCase(word.charAt(0)) and check if it's in "aeiou".
problems.hint3: Run both sequential and parallel and verify the counts match.
problems.sampleInput: parallel word count on "the cat the cat java"

problems.title: Fix the Broken Parallel Stream
problems.description:
Identify and fix a broken parallel stream that produces non-deterministic results:

**Broken:**
```java
List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
numbers.parallelStream().forEach(n -> result.add(n)); // NOT thread-safe!
```

**Fix options:**
1. `collect(toList())` instead of `forEach` + mutable list
2. Use `CopyOnWriteArrayList` or synchronized list
3. Use `.sequential()` for side-effect operations

Fixed: `numbers.parallelStream().collect(Collectors.toList())`
problems.hint1: ArrayList is not thread-safe — concurrent add() causes lost updates and ConcurrentModificationException.
problems.hint2: Replace forEach+add with collect(Collectors.toList()) — stream handles combining results safely.
problems.hint3: Never use shared mutable state in parallel stream operations. Use terminal collectors instead.
